html:
Dag Hjalmar Agne Carl Hammarskjöld, born on July 29, 1905, in Jönköping, and died on September 18, 1961, near Ndola in Northern Rhodesia (now Zambia), was a Swedish economist, lawyer, civil servant, diplomat, and author.

Hammarskjöld is primarily known for serving as the United Nations Secretary-General from 1953 until his death. He died while in office when the plane he was traveling in crashed in the jungle of Northern Rhodesia in September 1961. While theories immediately arose suggesting the plane had been shot down, initial investigations concluded that the crash was an accident. However, later attempts to clarify the circumstances surrounding Hammarskjöld's death have revealed information pointing towards the possibility that the incident was actually a murder.

In 1954, Hammarskjöld was appointed to seat 17 of the Swedish Academy, succeeding Hjalmar Hammarskjöld who had died the year before. He thus became the first member of the academy to succeed his parent.

Hammarskjöld was posthumously awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1961.

== Biography ==

=== Early Years ===

Dag Hammarskjöld was born in the noble family of Hammarskjöld at Villa Liljeholmen on Östra Storgatan in Jönköping in 1905, as the fourth and youngest son of Hjalmar Hammarskjöld and Agnes, née Almquist (1866–1940), and grandson of the director general Gustaf Fridolf Almquist, who was a half-brother to the writer Carl Jonas Love Almqvist. Dag Hammarskjöld was also the brother of the provincial governor Bo Hammarskjöld and nephew of Carl Gustaf Hammarskjöld. His other brothers were Åke Hammarskjöld and Sten Hammarskjöld (1900–1972).

Shortly after Dag Hammarskjöld's birth, his father, Hjalmar Hammarskjöld, was appointed a diplomat in Copenhagen and became the governor of Uppsala County in 1907. Hammarskjöld mainly grew up at Uppsala Castle, which he considered his childhood home. His father also served as Prime Minister from 1914 to 1917.

Even as a child, Hammarskjöld showed intellectual inclinations; he collected pressed plants, butterflies, and stuffed animals. He began school in 1911 at the private school Tomtebo, located at Villavägen 3 in Uppsala, where Hildur Akselsson (1887–1944) was his teacher. She wrote in a letter to his mother that it was a pleasure to teach him and that he had a great aptitude for learning. In 1916, he enrolled at Uppsala Higher All-Purpose School, which was located in the Cathedral School. He graduated in 1923 with eight A's, four B's, and one C (in gymnastics), and subsequently began his academic studies at Uppsala University; he initially studied literary history with poetics, philosophy, and French, which were part of his Bachelor of Arts degree in 1925. He then studied economics and finally social sciences and law.

Hammarskjöld obtained a Master's degree in Philosophy from Uppsala University in 1928. Due to the family's tradition of public service and some resistance from the professor of economics, he also decided to pursue a Bachelor of Laws degree there (1930). In 1933, he became a docent and in 1934, he obtained a doctorate in economics from Stockholm University. When he defended his dissertation, "Konjunkturspridningen: en teoretisk och historisk undersökning" (an appendix to the unemployment investigation) at the Social Institute on November 4, 1933, he broke with tradition by not wearing a tailcoat, becoming the first Swedish economist to do so.

=== Career in Sweden === In 1930, Dag Hammarskjöld became the secretary of the unemployment investigation of 1927, and he himself took the initiative to develop several appendices to the investigation, some of which later became academic dissertations. Within the investigation, he collaborated with people like Ernst Wigforss and Gösta Bagge. In 1932, Hammarskjöld was hired as a clerk in the Ministry of Finance after Wigforss became Minister of Finance in September. Based on recommendations from people including Bagge, he was employed from 1935 to 1941 as a secretary at the National Bank of Sweden, where he worked closely with the Governor of the National Bank, Ivar Rooth, who later became head of the International Monetary Fund in Washington. Hammarskjöld, being the youngest ever, was employed from 1936 to 1945 as a secretary in the Ministry of Finance, and from 1941 to 1948 as the chairman of the National Bank Board. He also held international positions early on. As early as 1944, he became a delegate in economic negotiations primarily with Great Britain and the United States, a position he held until 1948. From 1948 to 1953, he was part of the main delegation at OEEC and served for one year (1948-1949) as vice-chairman of its executive committee, and in 1950 he chaired the Swedish delegation's cooperation with Uniscana until 1953. In 1947 and the following year, he participated in the delegation at the Paris Conference. Hammarskjöld was also a prominent economist, belonging to the Stockholm School along with Gunnar Myrdal and Bertil Ohlin, and in 1946 he became an expert at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs on financial matters. As an economist, he was a member of the board of the National Institute for Economic Research from 1937 onwards for eleven years. He is considered to be the one who coined the term "planned economy." He played a significant role in shaping Swedish economic policy during his time in Sweden. Hammarskjöld served as a cabinet secretary from 1949 to 1951 at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, and later as a consultative minister (deputy foreign minister for economic affairs).

How certain are we about this?

People from the 20th century are extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns the facts themselves, but rather the interpretation and evaluation of those facts, which can change over time and depending on the perspective.
tldr: Dag Hammarskjöld was a Swedish economist and legal expert who served as the Secretary-General of the United Nations. He is best known for his work at the UN and for tragically dying in a plane crash while on assignment.
lattlast:
Dag Hammarskjöld was born in 1905 in Jönköping and grew up in a family with strong traditions in politics and public service. He studied at Uppsala University, where he became an expert in economics and law. He was a highly talented individual who early on secured important positions in Swedish finance, including roles at the National Bank and the Ministry of Finance.

He worked extensively on planning how Sweden's economy would function after World War II. He was an important advisor to the government and helped create regulations for managing money and trade. He also became known as the person who coined the term "planned economy."

In 1953, he received a very important international assignment: he became Secretary-General of the United Nations (UN), an organization where countries around the world cooperate. He remained in this role until his death in 1961 when his airplane crashed in Africa. After his death, he was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for his work promoting world peace.
